pattern: A cell can be covered by some fixed-size stamp exactly when the rectangle of that size anchored at every position overlapping it is itself free of occupied cells, so prefix sums plus a 2D difference array answer every cell in one pass.
hints: For a fixed stamp size, whether a given rectangle can host a stamp depends only on whether that rectangle contains any occupied cell -- a 2D prefix sum answers that check in constant time per candidate position.

Instead of asking cell-by-cell 'is this cell covered', flip the question: for every position where a stamp legally fits without touching an occupied cell, mark that its whole footprint becomes coverable, using a 2D difference array so each placement is an O(1) update.

Turn the difference array back into an actual per-cell coverage count with another 2D prefix sum, then the answer is simply: every originally empty cell must end up with a coverage count greater than zero.

edge_inputs.description: vertical occupied strip, two overlapping stamps cover the rest
diagonal occupied cells block every possible placement from reaching a corner
single empty cell, minimum grid and minimum stamp both 1x1
single occupied cell, no empty cells so the answer is vacuously true
grid entirely occupied, still vacuously true since there is nothing left to cover
a single occupied cell dead center blocks every candidate rectangle, leaving corner cells uncoverable
1x1 stamp can always cover any lone empty cell regardless of layout
single row, stamp width equal to the full row length
stamp height covers only part of the grid so a lower row's empty corner cells are missed

stress.description: small sparse random grids so the brute-force oracle can verify every candidate placement directly
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, 15)
    n = rng.randint(1, 15)
    density = rng.uniform(0.0, 0.15)
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < density else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    stamp_h = rng.randint(1, m)
    stamp_w = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [grid, stamp_h, stamp_w]
stress.note: low occupied density and small dimensions keep this near the usually-true regime while staying brute-force-checkable

stress.description: small denser random grids to stress the usually-false regime
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, 15)
    n = rng.randint(1, 15)
    density = rng.uniform(0.15, 0.45)
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < density else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    stamp_h = rng.randint(1, m)
    stamp_w = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [grid, stamp_h, stamp_w]
stress.note: higher occupied density makes stamp placements scarce, exercising the false path; grid stays small so the brute-force oracle stays fast
